posts.title: 5 Things That Should NEVER Be in Your Bedroom
posts.caption:
I have responded to bedroom fires in houses where every hazard on this list was already in place. People don't set them up deliberately — they accumulate over years without a second thought.

Extension cords under rugs. A candle left burning. A space heater three inches from a curtain. A power strip running six devices. A phone charging under the pillow.

Each one alone is manageable. Together, they turn a bedroom from a place of rest into a place that starts fires.

The fixes are not complicated. They are just habits you haven't built yet.

Free home safety checklist in bio.

posts.title: Why I Sleep With My Bedroom Door Closed Every Night
posts.caption:
Every night before I sleep, the door closes. It has been that way for twenty years. My wife thought it was strange when we first met. She doesn't anymore.

A closed door is not a superstition. It is a physics barrier between you and the two most dangerous things in a house fire: heat and smoke. Both travel fast. Both kill before flames ever get close.

You will still hear your alarm. Your family will still be safe. The cost is zero and the return is enormous.

One habit. Every night. Free home safety checklist in bio.

posts.title: The Lithium-Battery Rule Every Household Needs
posts.caption:
Lithium battery fires are not like ordinary fires. They generate their own oxygen. Water makes them worse. A standard home extinguisher is largely ineffective on a battery in thermal runaway.

The only winning move is to never let the fire start.

I have responded to apartment fires started by e-bikes, scooters, power tool packs, and laptop batteries. Every single one was preventable. Every single one followed the same pattern: wrong location, wrong charger, or ignored warning signs.

These five rules prevent all of them. Free home safety checklist in bio.

posts.title: If You Grill on a Balcony, Read This First
posts.caption:
Every summer the calls come in. Balcony fires. Sometimes one unit. Sometimes an entire building facade.

The grill is almost always the starting point. Usually charcoal. Usually close to the wall. Almost always unattended when it flared.

Grilling on a balcony is not inherently dangerous if you follow the rules. Distance. Supervision. Preparation. Knowledge of what your building actually allows.

The problem is that most people light up without checking any of those boxes first. Free home safety checklist in bio.

posts.title: Three Reasons Your Fire Extinguisher Is Useless
posts.caption:
An extinguisher mounted on a wall does not make your home safer if nobody knows how to use it, it's past its service date, or the pressure gauge is sitting in the red.

I've been on scenes where someone grabbed an extinguisher in a panic and couldn't get it to work. Not because of the fire. Because they had never held one before that moment.

PASS takes thirty seconds to learn. An expired unit takes two minutes to replace. A well-placed extinguisher takes one conversation to get right.

Make yours a tool, not a decoration. Free home safety checklist in bio.

posts.title: Carbon Monoxide Signs You'd Ignore — And Shouldn't
posts.caption:
Carbon monoxide has no smell. No color. No taste. The only warning you get is your own body starting to fail — and by then, you may not be able to act on it.

I have responded to CO incidents where the family had been exposed for days. Headaches they blamed on stress. Nausea they attributed to something they ate. A dog that hadn't been itself for a week.

Every single symptom was carbon monoxide.

A working detector on every level is not optional. It is the single most important thing between you and a silent killer. Free home safety checklist in bio.

posts.title: What Every Parent Needs to Teach Their Kid by Age 7
posts.caption:
I have thought about that call for twenty years. The house was fully involved by the time we arrived. Two of the three children made it out on their own. The third was found in her closet.

She was six years old. She had never been told not to hide.

That's not a failure of equipment. It's a failure of a conversation that takes five minutes to have.

I spent two years documenting everything I know about home fire safety — the habits, the rules, the things I wish every family already knew. It became a checklist. It's in my bio for nineteen dollars.

If it starts one conversation in your house, it was worth it.

posts.title: 5 Outlets in Your Home That Are Working Too Hard
posts.caption:
Electrical fires rarely start with a dramatic event. They start with years of sustained heat in wiring that was never designed to carry the load being put through it.

The insulation degrades slowly. The connections loosen. And then one night, at an unremarkable moment, something gives.

The five locations in this post are where I see overloaded circuits most often. Kitchen counter. Home office. Living room wall. Bedroom charging cluster. Garage.

Walk through your home today. If any of these describe what you have — spread the load. Free home safety checklist in bio.

posts.title: The Kitchen Rule My Wife and I Never Break
posts.caption:
We have one rule in our kitchen and we have never broken it: if you walk away from a hot stove, you turn the burner off first.

Not pause it. Not turn it low. Off.

It takes one second. It eliminates the number one cause of kitchen fires in residential buildings. And after enough repetitions, it stops feeling like a rule and starts feeling like the obvious thing to do.

The kitchen is where most home fires begin. The habits you build there are the firebreak between an inconvenience and a catastrophe. Free home safety checklist in bio.

posts.title: How a $4 Mistake Burned Down a Family Home
posts.caption:
The most common cause of fires is not a dramatic failure. It is something small that was already wrong — a bulb too bright for the fixture, a lint trap that hadn't been cleaned in weeks, a cord that had been bent in the same spot every day for a year.

These are not freak accidents. They are predictable outcomes of small neglected things.

I have been inside enough homes to tell you: the fires that could have been prevented cheapest are also the ones that cause the most damage, because nobody saw them coming.

Walk through your home today. Look for the four-dollar problem. Free home safety checklist in bio.

posts.title: The Bathroom Outlet Test That Takes 5 Seconds
posts.caption:
Most people have a GFCI outlet in their bathroom and have never tested it. Some of them failed years ago. The outlet still supplies power — it just no longer cuts it off when current takes an unintended path.

That is the definition of a false sense of security.

Five seconds per outlet. One press of the TEST button. If the outlet loses power, it works. If it doesn't, call an electrician.

Do this for every GFCI in your home — bathrooms, kitchen, garage, outdoors. Mark it on your calendar for the first of every month. Free home safety checklist in bio.

posts.title: 5 Things in Your Laundry Room You're Storing Wrong
posts.caption:
The laundry room gets overlooked because it doesn't feel dangerous. It is quiet. It is routine. You put clothes in, you take them out.

But it is a room that combines sustained heat, flammable compounds in the sheets and detergents, and one of the highest-risk appliances in a residential home.

Dryer fires are the fourth most common residential fire. The exhaust duct is almost always the origin point. The fix takes one professional cleaning per year.

Start there. Free home safety checklist in bio.
